title: Re-covering a padded armchair back panel
subtitle: Remove old fabric, replace damaged wadding, and re-staple fresh cover fabric to an armchair back
excerpt: How to strip and re-cover the outside back panel of an upholstered armchair, covering safe staple removal, assessment and replacement of Dacron wadding, cutting the new cover fabric, and back-tacking for a clean finished top edge.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Upholstery. BFTA upholstery repair guidance. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin card or plastic strip stapled to the frame along the top edge of a panel before the cover fabric is laid over it. The fabric folds back over the strip, which creates a straight, hard-edged fold line on the external face without any visible staples. Used at the top of armchair and sofa back panels and at the top of cushion borders. The strip is hidden inside the fold once the fabric is pulled down and fixed.

content.text: The outside back of an armchair is the one panel with a fully exposed, visible top edge, the line where the back fabric meets the top border or scroll must be straight and sharp. This is achieved with a
at the top edge. The sides and bottom are slip-stitched or stapled through to the inside of the frame and are not visible in use.

Cover fabric, cut 100 mm larger than the back panel in all directions. Dacron wadding if the existing padding needs replacing. Back-tack strip (cardboard tack strip or proprietary plastic strip). Cambric or black bottoming fabric to neaten the inside back if exposed. 10 mm T50 staples.

Work around the perimeter of the back panel with the upholstery staple remover, levering each staple fully out of the frame before moving to the next. Leaving staple legs in the frame causes the new fabric to lie unevenly over the stumps. Peel back the old fabric and the underlying calico or Dacron layer. Note the construction of the original, the order of layers, the position of the back-tack strip, before discarding.
Step 2, assess and replace the Dacron

If the original Dacron is matted, torn, or discoloured, cut a fresh layer to the panel size and staple it lightly to the back frame. The Dacron provides the softness that prevents the fabric from looking flat and hard against the frame rails. Cut the replacement layer 20 mm smaller than the panel on all sides so it does not bunch at the edges.

Lay the cover fabric face-up against the back frame, with the top edge of the fabric aligned with the top edge of the chair back rail. Place the back-tack strip on top of the fabric along this top line and staple through the strip and fabric into the top rail at 30 mm intervals. Fold the fabric down over the strip so it now faces downward, the right side of the fabric is against the chair back at this stage. Pull the fabric over the bottom rail and tension it evenly, stapling to the inside of the frame.
Step 4, pull and fix the sides

Pull the fabric firmly over each side rail and staple to the inside of the frame. Use the upholstery regulator through the weave to straighten any grain lines that have pulled off-square before driving the side staples. At the corners, fold the fabric into a neat pleat, fold the raw edge under, and staple. Trim any excess fabric to within 15 mm of the staple line.

attrs.symptom: Horizontal lines in the fabric showing across the back panel
attrs.cause: The fabric was not pulled with even tension downward before the side fixings were made, leaving slack at mid-height.
attrs.fix: Remove the side staples and re-tension the fabric by pulling more firmly at the midpoint of each side before re-stapling.

attrs.symptom: Top fold line is not straight across the full width
attrs.cause: The back-tack strip was not aligned straight with the top rail, or the staples along the strip were not at a consistent depth.
attrs.fix: The back-tack approach is forgiving: if the fold line is wavy, remove the fabric and strip, re-staple the strip with a straight line (use a pencil line on the rail as a guide), and re-fold.
